title: Fed holds rates steady, signals only one cut likely this year as inflation proves sticky
topicContext: EUR/USD reacts strongly to U.S. monetary policy decisions and Fed forward guidance. Hawkish Fed surprises (more hikes, fewer cuts, higher terminal rate) typically push EUR/USD lower as the rate differential favors the dollar. Dovish surprises do the opposite. The pair is currently trading in a 1.0820-1.0920 range. The European Central Bank has signaled it intends to start cutting rates in June, which would create a divergence story between Fed and ECB and weigh on EUR.
body:
The Federal Reserve held its benchmark interest rate unchanged in a range of 5.25% to 5.50% on Wednesday and reduced its projection for rate cuts this year to just one, down from three in its previous forecast, as inflation has proven more persistent than policymakers had expected.

The decision was unanimous and largely expected, but the so-called dot plot — a chart showing individual policymakers' rate projections — was significantly more hawkish than most analysts had forecast. The median projection now shows just one quarter-point cut by year-end, with policymakers also raising their estimates for inflation and economic growth in 2026.

In his post-meeting press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell said the central bank needs 'greater confidence that inflation is moving sustainably toward 2 percent' before lowering borrowing costs. Powell pointed to recent stronger-than-expected readings on consumer prices and wages, and said that progress on inflation had 'largely stalled' over the first three months of the year.

'We are prepared to hold rates at restrictive levels for as long as appropriate,' Powell said. He declined to say whether a rate cut at the September meeting was on the table, repeating that decisions would be made meeting by meeting based on incoming data.

Markets reacted sharply to the hawkish shift. The dollar index jumped 0.8% to a five-month high. Two-year Treasury yields rose to 4.96%, the highest level since November. The S&P 500 closed down 1.2%, with rate-sensitive sectors including real estate and utilities leading the declines.

The Fed's updated projections also showed an upward revision to the so-called neutral rate of interest — the level at which monetary policy is neither stimulative nor restrictive — to 2.8% from 2.6% previously, suggesting policymakers now believe rates will need to stay higher for longer in the long run.

The European Central Bank, by contrast, has signaled it intends to begin cutting interest rates at its meeting in June, even if the Fed remains on hold. ECB President Christine Lagarde said last week that the eurozone's disinflation process is 'broadly on track' and that the bank would not 'pre-commit to any rate path' but was prepared to ease.

Analysts said the divergence between the two central banks is likely to weigh on the euro against the dollar. 'You now have a Fed that may not cut at all this year and an ECB that's about to start a cutting cycle in two months,' said one strategist at a London bank. 'That's a meaningful rate-differential story for EUR/USD.'
